text: US equity, bond and over-the-counter markets were closed on Friday June 19 for the Juneteenth federal holiday, with the NYSE and Nasdaq shut and Federal Reserve banking services not operating; normal trading resumes Monday June 22. The authoritative US risk-sentiment prints for the date are therefore the June 18 close carried through the weekend rather than fresh June 19 cash readings, and the live read on June 19 came from the open European and Asian sessions.

The last US risk-sentiment print carried into the holiday was a strong tech-led relief rally on June 18: the S&P 500 rose 1.08% to 7,500.58, the Nasdaq Composite gained 1.91% to 26,517.93, and the Dow added 0.14% (about 72 points) to 51,564.70. The rally came after the June 17 Fed-driven sell-off (the Fed meeting pointed to a possible late-2026 rate hike) and on US-Iran de-escalation as Washington ended its Iran blockade, framing June 19 as a pause-and-reassess session rather than a fresh leg higher.

European risk sentiment softened on June 19: the pan-European STOXX 600 closed about 0.2% lower, easing back from record highs hit earlier in the week but still holding a roughly 0.4% weekly gain, as investors reacted to the postponement of the US-Iran technical talks and fresh Israel-Hezbollah hostilities in southern Lebanon before the ceasefire took hold.

London's FTSE 100 fell 0.34% (about 35 points) to close at 10,364, with losses led by miners Antofagasta (-6.16%) and Fresnillo (-5.36%) plus Unite Group (-4.23%), partly offset by energy and defensives BP (+2.08%), Relx (+1.43%) and Informa (+1.40%) as risk appetite faded into the weekend.

Sector breadth in Europe was defensive-tilted and commodity-driven: basic resources/mining fell 2.1% as metals prices eased (Antofagasta -6.1%, Rio Tinto -2.57%, Anglo American -2.63%, Glencore -1.6%, and BHP -4.3% after flagging a roughly $2.3 billion impairment on its Jansen potash project), travel and leisure dropped 0.9% on the firmer oil, while energy advanced 1.3% (BP +2.81%, Shell higher) - a rotation consistent with a market re-pricing geopolitical and commodity risk rather than a broad risk-off.

UK 10-year gilt yields rose about 8 basis points to 4.83% (with the 2-year up more than 6 bps to a one-week high of 4.25%) after public-sector borrowing reached £23.3 billion in May - above expectations and the highest May reading since 2019 - while retail sales rebounded 1.2% and Labour's Andy Burnham won the Greater Manchester mayoral by-election, adding a fiscal-risk overlay to the European sentiment backdrop.

Asian sentiment was led by a record close in Tokyo: Japan's Nikkei 225 rose 0.28% to a fresh all-time high of 71,250.06, even as the broader Topix slipped 0.57% to about 4,044.96 in mixed trade, after data showed core inflation steady at 1.4%. For the week the Nikkei and Topix advanced roughly 8% and 4% respectively as the US-Iran peace agreement drove oil lower and eased inflation worries.

Vendor-drift disclosure: the authoritative Nikkei 225 cash close used here is 71,250.06 (+0.28%) on a CNBC/AP basis, while Trading Economics' real-time JP225 feed showed a higher quote (roughly 71,773 to an intraday record near 71,952.99, a +1.0% to +1.3% daily-change basis). The gap reflects index-provider real-time/intraday pricing versus official cash settlement, not a contradiction in direction; both agree the index set a fresh record. The cash close is treated as primary per the pipeline's vendor-drift discipline.

Brent crude was up 0.25% (about $0.20) at $80.05 a barrel by 10:55 AM ET on June 19, and WTI rose 0.33% to $76.85, in light holiday-thinned volume - but Brent remained on track for a weekly fall of more than 8% as the Israel-Hezbollah ceasefire and rising Strait of Hormuz tanker traffic outweighed lingering risk, with US sanctions on Iranian oil being lifted. Phil Flynn of Price Futures Group noted 'the backlog of ships can move quicker than some people think.'

subject.name: Ceasefire and Hormuz reopening
subject.event: Israel-Hezbollah ceasefire and Strait of Hormuz tanker restart

text: The Israel-Hezbollah ceasefire took effect at 4 PM local time (1300 GMT) on Friday after an earlier exchange of fire, and at least four tankers carrying crude, products and LPG entered the Strait of Hormuz heading for Iraqi Gulf ports. The southern (Omani-waters) and northern (Iranian-waters) routes appeared fully open, though the main central route still had an estimated 80 mines to clear - a partial, conditions-bound reopening that supported the week's oil-price relief while leaving execution risk on the table.

subject.name: June 19 dominant sentiment driver
subject.event: US-Iran interim deal signed; Switzerland talks postponed amid Lebanon flare-up

text: The dominant sentiment driver on June 19 was a relief-rally narrative intact but visibly tempered by execution risk: the US and Iran had signed an interim memorandum of understanding digitally to halt fighting and reopen the Strait of Hormuz (with Iran's leadership approving it despite reservations and US sanctions on Iranian oil being lifted), yet the technical follow-up talks scheduled for Switzerland on Friday were postponed and Vice President JD Vance delayed his trip after fighting flared in southern Lebanon, where Israeli strikes killed at least 18 people before the Israel-Hezbollah ceasefire took hold. Markets read this as the deal holding but its seamless execution not yet proven.

A hawkish counter-current ran through global rate sentiment: the Bank of Japan had raised its benchmark rate by 25 bps to a 31-year high of 1% on June 16 in a 7-1 vote and signaled further hikes. Data released Friday June 19 showed Japan's core CPI (ex-fresh food) up 1.4% year-on-year in May, in line with forecasts, while 'core-core' inflation eased to 1.8% from 1.9% and Tokyo core CPI sat at 1.3% - a fourth month below the BOJ's 2% target that complicates the pace of further tightening even as the BOJ diverges from a Fed now signaling hike risk.

The cross-asset risk map on June 19 was mixed-to-cautiously-risk-off into the weekend: European equities dipped (STOXX 600 -0.2%, FTSE 100 -0.34%) on mining weakness, Asia was split with Japan at a record high against broader regional caution, US cash markets were shut for Juneteenth (last print the June 18 tech-led rally), Brent eked out a small intraday gain but stayed down about 8% on the week, and UK gilt yields backed up on fiscal data. The picture was of a market pausing to re-price the durability and execution of the US-Iran deal rather than reversing the week's relief rally, with the BOJ's tightening adding a hawkish rates undercurrent.
